dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for an unconscious male reported in an upstairs apartment-priority 1 with Fire.
Hi, I'm Alex with the rescue squad. You're doing much better than reported, but still have chest discomfort and a headache, right?
Yes. Chest tightness and a headache started about 30 minutes ago after taking Nystatin.
Let's confirm your awareness. What is your name?
Richard.
Where are we now?
Richmond, Virginia.
What day is today?
Saturday, the 5th of December, I think.
Can you describe what led you to call 911 today?
I was feeling faint and then chest tightness started after taking Nystatin. I told my neighbor, and she called 911.
You open your eyes normally. Can you follow my finger with your eyes?
Yes, I can follow your finger without trouble.
How are you feeling physically now?
My headache is still bad, but I'm much better than when you arrived. Chest pressure is there, but no pain now.
Squeeze my fingers hard-go ahead and do it for me.
Giving it a good squeeze, there you go.
You're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event. GCS 15. No memory issues noted so far. Good.
You can talk normally. Your airway appears patent. Any issues with breathing or chest discomfort now?
No, breathing is fine. Just pressure, not pain, and no trouble moving air.
Let me get the pulse ox reading quickly now. Breathing appears regular and unlabored, correct?
SpO₂ is 100%. Respirations at 16 per minute. No labored breathing or accessory muscle use. Breathing sounds clear bilaterally.
Pulse is regular and strong. Can I quickly check your blood pressure now, Richard?
Blood pressure is 102/71. Pulse is 98 and regular. Circulation is intact. Peripheral pulses are present and strong in both arms and legs.
And other symptoms you're having-like headache, cramping, and diarrhea-anything else bothering you now?
Weakness and no appetite. I haven't eaten solid food in over a week now because of this.
When's the last time you had solid food?
It's been over a week since I had anything solid. Mainly just water and broth since the medication started acting up on me.
So when did this all begin for you today and what you think brought it about?
After my latest dose of Nystatin. I started with headaches, diarrhea, and cramping. Then came the chest pressure and faint feeling.
Ever had anything like this before or had similar kinds of issues with medications?

I had some side effects with the same medication after starting it the first time but not as bad as this. Hospital said it was a minor fungal issue on the lungs though. They gave me Nystatin for that. I'm taking it as prescribed, but it's gotten worse.
What's your date of birth, Richard?
April 2nd, 1962.
Height and weight?
5'11", about 160 pounds.
What does the chest tightness feel like at the moment?
Like a heavy weight. Doesn't hurt, just feels like something is pressing in there.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how would you rate the chest discomfort now?
Around an 8.
Let me quickly look at your skin. Any rashes or discoloration?
No, you can look if you want to.
Your skin looks normal-warm, dry, intact, and not pale. No signs of jaundice, rashes, or bruises.
Head and neck are fine. No deformities or swelling. Pupils are PEARL. How about the pupil size?
Pupils are 4 mm and equal. Reactive to light.
Neck is normal. No swelling, JVD, or neck vein distention. PMS is present and intact in extremities.
Ears, nose, and mouth normals-no obstruction or discharge in the nasal passages. Any sore throat or swallowing difficulty?
No, nothing right now.
For lung assessment, can I put the stethoscope on your chest?
Breath sounds are clear and equal bilaterally-very healthy. Chest rise is symmetric. No wheezes or crackles.
How's the belly right now? Any pain, tenderness, or rigidity?
It's soft and normal. No pain here.
Abdomen is soft and nontender. Bowel sounds are present. No signs of herniation or mass.
Urination and stool habits-nothing reported beyond the diarrhea.
Arms and legs look normal. Any movement or use issues in the limbs?
No problem at all. Was able to stand up from the couch by myself.
Limbs show good PMS-intact in all four limbs. No obvious deformities or swelling. Patient was ambulatory from couch to chair outside.
Extremities and back are clear. Vertebral column and flank appear normal.
Blood glucose is 93 mg/dL.
Pulse is 98 and regular.
Respirations 16 per minute.
Blood pressure is 102/71.
SpO₂ is 100%.
EKG shows sinus rhythm-no ectopic activity so far.
Need to run a 12-lead EKG now for confirmation. Okay with that?
Sure.
EKG lead placement is secure. Here's the 12-lead reading. Interpretation is normal sinus rhythm.
Taking 324 mg aspirin by mouth for the reported chest tightness and presumed mild cardiac concern-consent for this?
Yes, I'll take it.
It'll dissolve faster if you chew it.
Requiring IV access for hydration and treatment. Can we start this now with a 20G access in your left arm?
Yes, go ahead.
IV catheter placed in left forearm successfully. Already given 500 mL of normal saline.
IV fluids are running. It should help ease the dehydration and some of the discomfort.
Initial fluid bolus of 500 mL of normal saline running now through the IV. Still subjective improvement noted so far.
IV running correctly and secure.
CV ECG, 4-lead monitoring is on-confirming sinus rhythm.
Following protocol 2-1 for non-cardiac chest complaints and acute medication side effects.
6:44 PM vitals are all stable-consistently regular. No change in baseline.
At 7:01 PM, vitals are pulse 81, BP 107/72 with no ECG or SpO₂ due to equipment issues.
Transporting to St. Mary's ER.
Hospital ER has been contacted-provider is ready for your arrival.
You able to stand up and move to the stair chair? I or my partner can help if needed.
Yep, can do that.
Securing you slowly in the stair chair.
Gone outside and transferred to the stretcher.
Secured on the stretcher and ready for loading.
Ambulance loaded. Ride should take about 20 minutes.
Transportation started, non-emergent route in progress.
You are stable en route. No major issues on the way.
All set at the hospital. No transfer issues upon arrival. Hospital received you without incident.
Hospital staff has you and your documents. Everything is good to go.
Signature collected and care transfer is complete.
ER prepared to handle your medication effects and symptoms.
Ambulance now available for next call.
